headline: French City Discovers Life Beyond Billboards
meta_description: Grenoble banned billboards over a decade ago. Now trees grow where ads once stood, and the city is inspiring others.
summary_short: A decade-long effort to remove advertising from public spaces has transformed Grenoble into Europe's first billboard-free historic city.
body:
When Grenoble's incoming mayor announced in November 2014 that he would not renew the city's billboard contract, few imagined what would follow. By the next year, 326 advertising devices—columns, large panels, and so-called "lollipops"—spanning more than 2,000 square meters had vanished from the French city's streets. Grenoble became Europe's first city to ban billboards in public spaces.

Today, walking along Cours Lafontaine through Grenoble's historic center, the transformation is visible. Where concrete advertising blocks once stood, a spacious bike path now runs. Trees line both sides of the street. A counter on the path shows over 2,000 cyclists passing daily—more than 600,000 so far this year.

Gilles Namur, Grenoble's deputy mayor for quality of life, explains the reasoning plainly: "Our lives are now full of ads, consumerist imagery that have negative impacts on society."

Scientific research supports this concern. A review of 52 studies concluded that visual pollution can lower concentration and cause anxiety, fear, and lethargy. As exposure to advertisements has grown from roughly 500 daily in the 1970s to up to 5,000 today, cities worldwide face the question Grenoble answered: what do we want our public spaces to say?

More than 50 black walnut trees now shade Grenoble's sidewalks where billboards once stood. The only remaining signage celebrates nonprofits and cultural activities.
